does: This paper proposes a Bayesian approach to align language models with human preferences by directly modeling the distribution of disagreements across human raters rather than collapsing them into single labels. Instead of using majority voting or averaging to consolidate inconsistent human feedback, the method treats human preference disagreement as a fundamental feature of the reward learning problem. The Bayesian framework enables the reward model to capture the nuanced degrees of disagreement and heterogeneity in human preferences, producing a richer representation that acknowledges multiple valid perspectives simultaneously while quantifying the uncertainty and variability inherent in subjective feedback.
connects: This work builds on the established line of research in reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) and reward modeling for language model alignment, which has become central to training systems like InstructGPT and ChatGPT. It responds directly to a known limitation of standard reward models: the loss of information when aggregating multiple inconsistent human judgments through majority voting or averaging. The paper connects to broader work on handling label disagreement in machine learning, subjective crowdsourcing tasks, and probabilistic modeling of human preferences. It extends prior Bayesian approaches to reward learning by specifically addressing the distribution of human disagreement as an explicit modeling challenge rather than a noise problem to be minimized away.
enables: This approach enables more robust and representative reward models that can capture diverse human values and preferences rather than forcing consensus toward a single preference distribution. By explicitly modeling disagreement, the method could support downstream applications where understanding preference diversity is valuable, such as personalized language model deployment or multi-objective optimization that respects heterogeneous user preferences. The framework may also improve generalization by learning from the full spectrum of human feedback rather than artificially compressed consensus labels. Future work could leverage this disagreement-aware modeling for more nuanced human preference elicitation, preference discovery across demographic groups, and developing language models that transparently reflect value pluralism.
